titleStmt.title: Canto 2: ūralaruraittakātai
The Canto in which Gossip in the City is Reported (75 lines)

summary.p: 2.1-9 Mātavi's mother Cittirāpati, upset about the gossip that has arisen as a result of Maṇimēkalai's and her absence from the Indra Festival, summons Mātavi's confidante Vayantamālai to deliver a message to her.
2.10-17 Vayantamālai finds Mātavi and Maṇimēkalai in the pavillion. They are weakened by asceticism. She asks what has happened.
2.18-32 Vayantamālai lists Mātavi's attainments as a dancer, to remind her of her true calling.
2.33-36 Vayantamālai tells Mātavi that to avoid shaming gossip she must give up her asceticism.
2.37-41 Mātavi replies that since losing her lover Kōvalaṉ she has renounced shame.
2.42-48 Mātavi lists the options open to widows: spontaneous death, the pyre, or penance.
2.49-55b Mātavi cites Kaṇṇaki as a paragon of female virtue.
2.55c -57 Mātavi states her wish that Maṇimēkalai, who is Kaṇṇaki's daughter (by marriage), renounce the courtesan life and become a nun.
2.58-69 Mātavi recounts how, after the death of Kōvalaṉ, she went to a Buddhist teacher, Aṟavaṇa for instruction.

2.70-75 Mātavi concludes her speech to Vayantamālai with a request to report her decision to Cittirāpati and the other women. Having failed to convince Mātavi, Vayantamālai returns in despair to Cittirāpati.

div.p: The second song, in which gossip in the city is reported.

lg.l: nāva lōṅkiya māperun tīviṉuṭ
kāvaṟ teyvatan tēvarkōṟ keṭutta
tīvakac cānti ceytaru naṉṉāṇ
maṇimē kalaiyoṭu mātavi vārāt
taṇiyāt tuṉpan talaittalai mēlvarac
cittirā patitāṉ cellaluṟ ṟiraṅkit
tattari neṭuṅkaṭ taṉmaka ṭōḻi
vayanta mālaiyai varukeṉak kūuyp
payaṅkeḻu mānaka ralareṭut turaiyeṉa

lg.p: 1.1-9 (9 lines) The Aetiology of the Indra Festival: The Cōḻa King Cempiyaṉ, on the command of the sage Akattiyaṉ, requests Lord Indra to come to stay in the Cōḻa city of Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam for the 28 days of the Indra festival. Lord Indra consents.
lg.l: When the man of rare penance (the sage Akattiyaṉ) commanded it [from his seat on] towering Mount Potiyam—
in order that the qualities of the ancient city (Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam)
extolled by many people [and] of sublime, towering fame/beauty/superiority/prosperity unchanging [in the eyes] of the world
become [even more] widely known –
Cempiyaṉ (a Cōḻa king) of the shoulder-bangles, who destroyed the hanging fortresses,
standing bowed before the Lord of the Celestials (Indra), addressed [him]:
'May he who is [well] spoken of on earth (Indra)
dwell very sweetly in the/his heavenly city (Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam) for the twenty-eight days
during which the festival is held—since those on high (i.e. the gods) desire [it]!'
Thereupon the Lord of the Immortals (Indra) consented to that [request].
lg.p: 1.10-18ab (19 lines) The Vedic scholars of Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam approve the festival. Different groups—religious experts, astrologers, the gods disguised as humans, people of different linguistic groups, the king's ministers and retinue—pour into the city.
lg.l: [And] since the scholars of the ungraspable Vedas did not gainsay [this edict]—
the religious experts [of Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam], who show the nature of those
various different doctrines which deal with/which are known as:
epistemology, custom (i.e. secular literature?) [and] the good tenets of liberation;
and the astrologers (lit. time-counters), who follow their domain(s)
did not leave [the city];
and the gods, who had taken on [human] forms as a disguise,
and people of [different] tongues who had gathered together, spreading [everywhere in the city];
and the five great [royal] councils and the eight great groups [forming the royal retinue] arrived, crowding all together;
lg.p: 18cd-26 (9 lines) For fear of inciting the hostility of the bhūta, the assembled groups resolve to perform the festival.
lg.l: [And they] resolved: 'If [we] forget to hold the festival in [our] heavenly city,
the bhūta who freed the victorious-king (Mucukunta) with armies and chariots with banners from [his] misery
will cause [us] affliction with the roaring sound of thunder,
while the strong teeth in his red mouth with its pursed [lips] flash.
[This] bhūta, who will beat and eat the sinners of [our] ancient city whom it will capture in [its] noose, will become hostile [to us].
[Therefore] let the festival to the thousand-eyed one (Indra), in which the supreme monarchs of the great, vast earth are gathered, commence!'
lg.p: 1.27-31 The drummer beats the drum of Indra to summon Yama and proclaim the festival.
lg.l: The man born of an ancient clan beats with a stick the drum—
the union-drum (i.e. festival drum?) from the enclosure of the thunderbolt (i.e. Indra's temple),
mounted on the nape of an elephant [wearing] a belt,
wrapped in a bull hide, with a thunderous roar,
with blood-lust, summoning Yama—[and proclaims:]
'Long live the ancient city loved by Śrī!' declared [he] in exaltation.
'May rain showers be plentiful!
May the king be the holder of the [just] sceptre by which the planets do not change position!
lg.p: 1.35-42 The drummer's proclamation: Devaloka will be emptied as all the gods throng to Kāvirippūmpaṭṭiṉam.
lg.l: On the auspicious days on which the Island Propitation [Festival] is performed,
the four different [types] of dēva-s
who accompany the thousand-eyed one (Indra) there (i.e. to the city),
and the different divisions of dēva-s
who have very appropriate excellence,
will set out for this city;
because of the inference by those who understand the old ways that
[the gods] will leave so that the Golden City (Devaloka) will be [left] empty
just as this city was
on the day when King Karikāl Vaḷavaṉ departed—
lg.p: 1.43-67 The drummer's proclamation: adorn and prepare the city!
lg.l: In the streets with festoons and in the flawless/viceless assembly-halls
spread, many together, full water-pots, golden pots, and lamps [held by] girls/[shaped like] girls!
Set up [branches of] Areca-palm with clusters of unripe fruits, banana-trees and Mahua-trees,
flower creepers, and sugarcanes!
Hang in order garlands of pearls,
from top quality gold pillars on/with platforms in rows!
Change the old sand, spread new sand
in the squares and streets of the very ancient city in which the festival flourishes!
Fix at the gates and at the corniced mansions
large flags fixed to beams, and banners [made of] plantain-trees!
O [you] who know the tradition which is has six paths! Perform
the various different rituals [for the gods] which must be performed with various different special powers—
starting with the lord with the unblinking eye on his forehead (Śiva),
and ending with the deities of the crossroads who dwell in the city!
O you who know the good, sacred scriptures/commentaries on virtue, approach
the awnings [spread with] cool sand, and the public-halls which provide [places] to stay!
O debaters of the tenets in rivalling doctrines,
ascend tothe paṭṭi assembly hall, knowing [your] places!
Avoid and pass beyond aversion and rivalry—
even with [your] enemies!
O you who know well all the twenty eight days
during which gods and humans wander together as equals/in harmony—
on the white sand dunes and in the groves with opening flowers,
on the islets of cool sand and by the splendid low-lying/deep ghats!' thus proclaimed [the drummer].
While warriors with shining swords, chariots, horses
and male elephants surrounded [him], the drum with an eye beating,
[he] uttered the following benediction: 'Banishing hunger, disease and emnity,
may rain and prosperity increase!'
Thus he (the drummer), [standing] in the inner city, proclaimed the splendid festival.

gramGrp.gram: verbal root as peyareccam imperfective aspect

entry.def: one who counts/one who is versed in
one who counts/one who is versed in
inhabitants of hell, sinners as fit for hell

verbal root as peyareccam imperfective aspect

entry.def: set out to/set their thoughts on
